content.text: What It Is

Stickiness and engagement tracking measures the depth and durability of AI tool adoption after initial activation. While the adoption funnel (see the companion topic) tracks whether employees are reaching usage milestones, stickiness metrics track whether they are staying there and deepening their usage over time. A sticky AI tool is one that employees return to habitually because it reliably improves their work; a non-sticky tool is one that employees try, find mildly useful, and gradually deprioritise as novelty wanes.

Stickiness is typically quantified through engagement ratios borrowed from consumer product analytics: DAU/MAU (Daily Active Users divided by Monthly Active Users) is the canonical stickiness metric. A ratio of 0.2 means the average user engages with the tool on 20 % of working days — about one day per week. A ratio of 0.5 (three times per week) represents strong stickiness. Cohort-level stickiness analysis tracks these ratios for groups of users who started using the tool in the same month, enabling comparison of how different cohorts' engagement evolves over time and revealing whether programme interventions have improved stickiness for later cohorts.

Engagement tracking goes beyond frequency to capture depth: how long are sessions? How many interactions occur in each session? Are users trying a narrow set of tasks or exploring a broad range of AI capabilities? Are they using the tool's output downstream — pasting it into a document, sending it in an email, or connecting it to another workflow? These depth metrics reveal whether the tool is becoming genuinely integrated into the employee's work or whether it is being used in a superficial, exploratory way that is unlikely to produce business value.
Why It Matters

High initial trial rates combined with low stickiness reveal a fundamental problem in the AI adoption proposition: the tool is interesting enough to try but not valuable enough to habituate. This pattern is common in early AI deployments where tools are promoted with broad claims but provided without the use-case specificity, skill building, and workflow integration that make habitual use rewarding. Detecting this pattern early through stickiness tracking allows the programme team to intervene before the initial wave of interest dissipates into collective disillusionment.

Cohort-level stickiness comparison is one of the most powerful analytical tools in the adoption programme manager's arsenal. If cohort 3 (onboarded with a revised programme design) shows D30 retention of 72 % compared to cohort 1's 45 %, the improvement provides direct evidence that the programme change was effective. This data enables evidence-based programme iteration in a way that aggregate metrics cannot.
Benefits
Detects fading adoption early:
Stickiness metrics signal declining engagement 30-60 days before aggregate adoption rate statistics would reveal the problem.
Validates programme changes:
Cohort comparison provides direct evidence of whether programme design changes improved engagement durability.
Reveals depth of integration:
Session depth metrics show whether employees are integrating AI output into their downstream workflow or simply experimenting without application.
Predicts long-term ROI:
High-stickiness users produce disproportionate business value; stickiness is a leading indicator of ROI.
Identifies at-risk cohorts:
Early-cohort stickiness monitoring identifies groups whose engagement is declining before they fully disengage.
Enables re-engagement targeting:
Users whose stickiness metrics decline below threshold can be automatically targeted with re-engagement content.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Context-blindness:
Low frequency does not always mean low value; a weekly power user who saves 4 hours per session may deliver more value than a daily light user.
Role variation:
Stickiness thresholds that are appropriate for knowledge workers may be irrelevant for frontline roles with limited AI-amenable tasks.
Tool-imposed variability:
Stickiness is partly a function of the tool's design (notifications, integrations, habit-forming features) not just the employee's enthusiasm.
Gaming risk:
Employees aware that session frequency is being tracked may open the tool without meaningful use to avoid being flagged as disengaged.
Privacy sensitivity:
Session-frequency data at the individual level is sensitive; aggregate to team level for reporting.
How It Works

The primary stickiness metric is DAU/WAU or DAU/MAU depending on the tool's intended use frequency. For tools designed for daily use (a writing assistant, a meeting summariser), DAU/WAU is the most informative ratio. For tools intended for weekly use (a research assistant, a document drafter), WAU/MAU is more appropriate. Define the intended use frequency based on the use cases the tool is being deployed for, and set stickiness targets accordingly.

Retention curves are the second core stickiness metric. A retention curve plots the percentage of a cohort that remained active at D7, D14, D30, D60, and D90 after their first active session. Healthy retention curves for enterprise AI tools typically show D30 retention of 50-70 % and D90 retention of 35-55 %. Curves that drop sharply (below 30 % at D30) indicate a fundamental activation or value delivery problem; curves that are flat from D7 to D90 (indicating immediate habituation) are a positive signal of successful onboarding.

Session depth metrics augment frequency metrics with a quality dimension. Track: average session duration (a proxy for the complexity of tasks being attempted), average interactions per session (number of prompts sent and responses reviewed), feature breadth (number of distinct tool capabilities used per user in the last 30 days), and downstream usage (whether the AI output was copied, exported, or otherwise used in another application). Downstream usage is the most direct evidence of genuine integration: if AI output is not being used, the tool is not producing value regardless of how frequently it is accessed.

Cohort-level analysis compares stickiness metrics across employee groups defined by their start month, role category, business unit, onboarding programme version, or any other segmentation variable of interest. The most valuable comparison is across onboarding programme versions: if cohort 5 (onboarded with a revised 90-minute structured programme) shows significantly higher D30 retention than cohort 1 (onboarded with ad-hoc self-service), the data provides direct evidence for the programme investment.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Stickiness and engagement tracking: six dimensions from frequency ratios through retention curves to net promoter signals

content.text: The six engagement dimensions are layered from least to most sophisticated. DAU/WAU/MAU ratios are the most easily instrumented, requiring only session count data. Session depth requires richer telemetry (interaction logs, feature usage events). Retention curves require cohort construction (user-level first-session date) plus ongoing session tracking. Use-case breadth requires task classification — either from structured task categories in the tool or from NLP classification of prompt text. Cross-system integration requires integration telemetry (clipboard events, API calls to downstream systems). Net promoter signals are primarily survey-derived.
When to Use It

Start tracking stickiness from the first cohort's activation day. The most valuable stickiness data is the D7 and D14 retention curve, which provides early signal on whether onboarding is creating durable habits. If D7 retention falls below 40 %, the programme team has two weeks to identify and address the cause before the cohort's interest fully dissipates — a narrow but actionable window.
How to Implement
Define the stickiness metric suite for your tool's intended use frequency: daily-use tools → DAU/WAU; weekly-use tools → WAU/MAU; also track D7, D30, D60, D90 retention curves.
Build cohort tables: for each user, record their first-active-session date; this creates the cohort anchor from which all retention calculations are made.

Configure the telemetry pipeline to emit session-level events: session start, session duration, interaction count, feature events (which capabilities were used), and downstream usage events (copy, export, API calls).

Build retention curve visualisations: for each cohort, plot the % still active at D7, D14, D30, D60, D90; overlay curves from multiple cohorts on the same chart to reveal programme improvement over time.
Set stickiness alert thresholds: configure automated alerts when cohort D30 retention falls below 40 % or DAU/MAU ratio falls below 0.15 for any team segment.

Instrument downstream usage: where technically feasible, track whether AI-generated output is used in downstream applications (clipboard paste events, export events, or Copilot-generated content tagged in downstream documents).
Run monthly cohort reviews: compare current cohort stickiness to the previous two cohorts; identify whether programme changes are improving retention.
Combine stickiness data with ROI metrics: high-stickiness users should show disproportionate time savings and use-case deployment rates; validate this correlation quarterly.
Examples
Real-World Example

A media company rolled out an AI writing assistant to 600 content producers across three cohorts in H1 2025. Cohort 1 (January, ad-hoc self-service onboarding) showed D30 retention of 42 % and a DAU/WAU ratio of 0.18. Cohort 2 (March, 60-minute onboarding webinar) showed D30 retention of 54 % and DAU/WAU of 0.24. Cohort 3 (May, 90-minute structured programme with 30-day challenge) showed D30 retention of 71 % and DAU/WAU of 0.37.

The stickiness improvement from cohort 1 to cohort 3 translated directly into ROI: high-stickiness users (DAU/WAU > 0.3) saved an average of 5.2 hours per week of drafting and editing time, compared to 1.8 hours per week for low-stickiness users (DAU/WAU < 0.1). The programme team used this data to mandate 90-minute structured onboarding for all subsequent cohorts and to introduce a 30-day challenge for every new user — changes that lifted the programme-wide average from 1.8 to 3.9 hours saved per week across the full user population.
Leading vs. Lagging Stickiness Signals

Stickiness metrics can be classified into leading signals — early indicators that predict future engagement — and lagging signals — retrospective measures of established behaviour. Distinguishing these is important for programme timing: lagging signals confirm what has already happened; leading signals give the programme team enough advance notice to intervene.

The most reliable leading signals for enterprise AI stickiness are: session depth score in the first two weeks of access (users with high depth scores in week one are 3x more likely to reach regular-user status by D90); completion of structured onboarding (users who complete onboarding within 7 days of access are 2.8x more likely to reach activation than those who do not complete it within 30 days); and champion contact in the first 14 days (users who have at least one conversation with a champion in their first two weeks show consistently higher D30 retention). Monitor these leading signals weekly for every new cohort and trigger proactive re-engagement when any leading indicator falls below threshold.

Lagging signals — D30 retention rate, DAU/MAU ratio at 60 days, session depth score averaged over the first month — confirm whether the programme's leading-signal interventions worked. Review lagging signals monthly as a programme health check and use them for cohort comparison analysis to validate programme iteration decisions.
Engagement Scoring for Recognition Programmes

Stickiness data can feed into champion recognition programmes to identify and reward high-engagement teams. A team engagement score combines the team's DAU/MAU ratio (40 % weight), the team's average session depth score (30 % weight), and the number of distinct team members who reached the activated stage in the last 30 days (30 % weight). Publishing ranked team engagement scores — with appropriate privacy protections and manager notification — creates positive competitive pressure that has been shown to accelerate adoption in high-performing teams.
Monitor leading stickiness signals weekly:
week-one session depth, onboarding completion within 7 days, champion contact within 14 days.
Review lagging signals monthly:
D30 retention, DAU/MAU at 60 days, average session depth at 30 days.
Build team engagement scores:
combine frequency, depth, and activation breadth metrics for recognition programme integration.
Automate leading-signal alerts:
trigger proactive re-engagement when any new cohort's week-one depth score or onboarding completion rate falls below threshold.
Segment stickiness by intended use frequency:
daily-use tools → DAU/WAU; weekly-use tools → WAU/MAU; don't apply the same ratio to tools designed for different cadences.
Common Mistakes
Using only frequency metrics without depth:
A user who opens the tool daily for one interaction each visit may deliver less value than a user who uses it deeply twice per week.
Not segmenting stickiness by role:
Average stickiness across all roles masks the fact that some roles have far higher natural frequency than others.
No cohort comparison:
Without comparing cohorts, programme improvements cannot be attributed to specific changes; you cannot learn from your own interventions.
Reporting individual stickiness scores:
Individual-level engagement data feels like surveillance to employees; aggregate to team level for all external reporting.
Ignoring retention curve shape:
A D30 retention of 50 % looks the same whether users dropped out at D7 or at D28; the curve shape reveals when and how quickly disengagement occurs.
Best Practices
Track both frequency stickiness (DAU/MAU) and depth stickiness (session depth score) as complementary dimensions.
Build retention curves from the first cohort; early curves provide the baseline for all future programme comparisons.
Set a D30 retention alert threshold (suggested: 40 %); investigate any cohort that falls below this threshold immediately.
Run cohort comparison analysis quarterly; use it as the primary evidence base for programme iteration decisions.
Downstream usage (export rate) is the single most valuable depth metric — it is the closest available proxy for genuine tool integration.
Correlate stickiness with ROI metrics quarterly to validate that high-stickiness users are delivering proportional business value.
Performance and Scalability

Stickiness calculations at 1,000+ users require a proper data warehouse architecture. Store raw session events in a time-partitioned table; build cohort tables as a derived layer; compute daily and weekly aggregates in scheduled batch jobs. Retention curve computation is computationally inexpensive but requires careful join logic — match each user's sessions to their cohort start date and compute the retention window accurately. For very large programmes (50,000+ users), consider pre-computing cohort aggregates daily using a tool like dbt and Snowflake rather than computing on-demand in the dashboard layer.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Tool
Purpose
Notes
Snowflake / BigQuery
Session data warehouse
Partitioned storage for high-volume session events
dbt
Cohort and retention calculation logic
SQL transformations; version-controlled; testable
Amplitude
Product analytics with built-in cohort retention
Best for custom AI tools; less suitable for M365 platforms
Power BI
Retention curve visualisations
Custom retention curve visuals available via AppSource
Python (pandas)
Ad-hoc cohort analysis
Flexible for one-off analyses; not suitable for production reporting
Slack / Teams bots
Re-engagement nudges
Trigger re-engagement sequences when stickiness drops below threshold
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strength
Weakness
DAU/MAU stickiness ratio
Industry standard; easy to compare
Context-blind; high-value weekly users look poor
Retention curve analysis
Reveals when users disengage
Requires cohort infrastructure; more complex to build
Session depth scoring
Captures quality not just frequency
Requires richer telemetry; harder to instrument
Manager-reported engagement
Captures context not in telemetry
Subjective; inconsistent; not scalable
Security and Reliability

Session frequency and depth data at the individual level constitutes employee monitoring data in many jurisdictions. Before implementing stickiness tracking, consult your DPO and employment law counsel to confirm that the tracking is permissible under local law, that employees have been notified through appropriate channels (works council agreement in Germany, France, and other co-determined jurisdictions), and that data is retained no longer than necessary. All reporting should be at team or role-segment level, with individual-level data accessible only to the data engineering team for pipeline operation and debugging.
Key Takeaways
Stickiness tracks depth and durability of AI tool adoption — not just whether employees tried it, but whether they kept using it and integrated it into their work.
DAU/MAU ratios and cohort retention curves are the two primary stickiness metrics; track both from the first cohort's activation day.
Session depth (especially downstream output usage / export rate) is the closest available proxy for genuine workflow integration.
Cohort comparison analysis is the most valuable use of stickiness data; it reveals whether programme design changes are improving engagement durability.
Set a D30 retention alert threshold and investigate immediately when any cohort falls below it.
Individual-level stickiness data is employment monitoring data; aggregate to team level for all reporting and configure appropriate access controls.
